ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for soy-braised egg; closest parent is hen egg food product (FOODON_03302184).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked hen eggs broadly; no specific code for soy-braised preparation exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 is the closest parent concept for egg-based food; no specific braised egg concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One braised egg weighing approximately 50 g, typically served as a side dish or topping for noodles and rice dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01129)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled whole egg (NDB 01129 / FDC 173424). Sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce braising marinade absorption, consistent with published estimates for soy-braised egg preparations. Soy sauce contribution to sodium estimated at approximately 150–200 mg per 100 g of finished product based on typical braising ratios reported in food composition literature. All other macros retained from USDA hard-boiled egg baseline.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggs are a protein- and fat-dominant food with negligible digestible carbohydrate (1.8 g per 100 g, of which the braising marinade contributes minor sugar). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) consistently classify eggs as having a GI of effectively 0 due to the absence of glycemic carbohydrates. Per Veritas v2 rules for effectively zero-GI foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (1 × 0.9) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce braising marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit braised egg consumption or request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparations, as a single 50 g egg may contribute approximately 240 mg sodium, representing about 10–16% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised egg has a negligible glycemic index and glycemic load due to its very low carbohydrate content, making it a suitable protein-rich food choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion control remains advisable given the moderate fat and cholesterol content.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Braised egg contains approximately 373 mg cholesterol per 100 g and elevated sodium from soy sauce braising; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption in line with current dietary guidelines that recommend limiting dietary cholesterol and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised egg (卤蛋, Lǔ Dàn) is a classic Chinese preparation in which a hard-boiled chicken egg is simmered in a fragrant braising liquid composed of soy sauce, water, sugar, five-spice powder, star anise, garlic, and ginger. The prolonged braising process infuses the egg white with a deep mahogany colour and a savoury-sweet umami flavour, while the yolk may range from fully set to slightly jammy depending on the initial boiling time. The texture of the white becomes firm and slightly chewy from the soy marinade, while the yolk retains a creamy, rich quality. Braised eggs are a staple street food and hawker dish across China, Taiwan, and Singapore, commonly served as a side dish, topping for noodle soups, rice dishes, or congee, or sold individually as a snack. Nutritionally, braised egg is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with negligible carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-glycaemic dietary patterns. It is a meaningful source of vitamin B12, vitamin A, selenium, phosphorus, and choline. The primary nutritional concern is its elevated sodium content from the soy sauce braising liquid and its high dietary cholesterol, which are relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, braised egg is a low-glycaemic food with a glycaemic load of effectively zero per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a braised egg?
Is braised egg good for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of soy sauce egg?
Can I eat braised egg if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between braised egg and ramen egg?
What nutrients are in a braised egg?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled egg data (a well-validated primary source) with sodium adjusted to reflect soy sauce braising absorption. No exact braised egg entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a distinct prepared dish. The adaptation is methodologically sound but not an exact match to a primary source entry for this specific preparation.
notes: Sodium value of 480 mg per 100 g is an estimate reflecting the base hard-boiled egg sodium (~124 mg per 100 g per USDA) plus an estimated 150–200 mg per 100 g contribution from soy sauce marinade absorption during braising, consistent with food composition literature on soy-braised preparations. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g as eggs contain no dietary fibre; this is naturally zero for an egg-based food. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg as eggs contain negligible vitamin C; this is naturally zero. Trans fat is 0.0 g as confirmed by USDA SR Legacy data for hen eggs. GI is set to 1 (effectively zero) because eggs contain no glycemic carbohydrates; this is confirmed zero per University of Sydney GI Database classification of egg foods; gi_category is 'low' per convention. GL per 50 g serving = 0.0. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 173424 (hard-boiled egg). No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as USDA SR Legacy provides comprehensive micronutrient data for hard-boiled egg. Halal status is assumed as no pork or alcohol ingredients are required; however, specific restaurant preparations should be verified. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' is preserved from the input record as eggs are conventionally grouped here in Singapore dietary classification systems.
